matches.rationale: The end-to-end allowance computation workflow — collect eligibility data from site systems, apply rules, flag exceptions, route to approvers, update payroll — is a multi-step process currently executed manually by HR and finance staff. A task automation agent can orchestrate this pipeline across systems, with branching logic for approvals and error handling for missing data, replacing the error-prone manual handoffs that cause late and incorrect payments.
matches.sketch: An allowance processing agent runs each pay cycle: pulls staff records, evaluates eligibility rules, computes amounts, routes edge cases to a human approver queue, and pushes confirmed entries to the payroll system — with a full audit log of decisions and approvals.

painPoints.label: Leave Accrual & Utilization Opacity

matches.rationale: The root confusion stems from DepEd rules and institutional policy never being reconciled into a single plain-language document. AI text generation can synthesize the regulatory source text, institutional HR policies, and carry-over rules into a clear, authoritative leave policy handbook that staff can actually understand. This directly addresses the 'no clear statement of accrual' and 'carry-over rules unclear' pain mechanisms.
matches.sketch: A leave policy document generator that ingests DepEd circulars plus the school's internal HR manual and outputs a unified, plain-language leave guide covering VL, SL, BL, EL — with worked examples of accrual math, carry-over tables, and separation payout rules.

matches.rationale: Principals currently track leave on paper or spreadsheets with inconsistent schemas and mixed DepEd/institutional leave type codes. AI-assisted data transformation can normalize these disparate inputs — cleaning leave type labels, mapping fiscal vs. calendar year entries, standardizing principal-level spreadsheets — into a single structured ledger schema. This directly attacks the 'no centralized leave ledger' and 'untracked liabilities' mechanisms.
matches.sketch: A one-time (and recurring) ETL pipeline that accepts principal spreadsheets and paper-digitized records, maps them to a canonical leave schema (leave type, period, balance, carry-over flag), and populates a central leave ledger — surfacing liability totals immediately.

matches.rationale: Teachers and principals repeatedly ask the same leave-rule questions — 'how many SL days do I have left?', 'can I carry over VL to next year?', 'what happens to my unused EL on resignation?' — and get inconsistent answers from HR. A RAG system grounded in DepEd circulars and the school's own policy documents can answer these questions accurately and consistently, reducing grievances and HR burden. This directly addresses staff frustration and dispute frequency.
matches.sketch: An HR knowledge assistant (web or messaging app) where teachers query their leave entitlements, carry-over rules, and separation payouts; the system retrieves the relevant DepEd/institutional policy passages and synthesizes a cited, accurate answer.

matches.rationale: Untracked leave obligations represent a financial liability risk that surfaces acutely at separation or audit. A risk scoring model can estimate total leave liability exposure per site and flag schools where principal-tracked balances are likely materially understated — e.g., based on headcount, tenure distribution, and historical leave utilization patterns. This converts an opaque liability into a quantified, prioritized risk register for finance and HR leadership.
matches.sketch: A leave liability risk model that scores each campus on estimated undeclared leave obligation based on staff tenure and leave-type mix, helping the CFO triage which sites need urgent ledger reconciliation before the next separation cycle.

matches.rationale: The leave lifecycle — application → principal approval → HR recording → balance update → carry-over calculation at year-end → separation payout computation — is a multi-step workflow currently done manually across paper, spreadsheets, and email. A task automation agent can orchestrate this entire workflow: ingest leave requests, apply accrual rules, update the ledger, flag carry-over decisions, and compute separation liabilities automatically. This attacks the untracked liability risk and the manual principal burden simultaneously.
matches.sketch: An automated leave management pipeline: teacher submits request → agent validates against entitlement rules → principal approves via simple UI → ledger updates → year-end carry-over agent runs rule-based cutoff → separation trigger fires payout calculation for finance.

painPoints.label: Weak or Opaque Benefits Package

matches.rationale: The pain includes staff uncertainty about entitlements — a communication and clarity problem. AI can generate clear, plain-language benefits summaries, FAQ documents, and onboarding materials that explain exactly what staff are entitled to under GSIS, PhilHealth, and any supplemental benefits. This directly addresses the 'staff unsure of what they're entitled to' dimension of the pain.
matches.sketch: An AI-assisted benefits communication toolkit that auto-generates personalized entitlement summaries per employment type (full-time, part-time, probationary) and answers the most common questions in plain Filipino/English, reducing HR inquiry load.

matches.rationale: Staff frequently don't know what benefits they are entitled to, and HR teams spend significant time fielding repetitive entitlement questions. A RAG system grounded in the school's benefits documents, GSIS regulations, and labor code provisions can answer specific staff queries accurately and instantly, reducing HR burden and improving staff confidence. This directly addresses the opacity dimension of the pain.
matches.sketch: A staff-facing benefits Q&A chatbot that answers questions like 'Am I eligible for maternity leave top-up?' or 'What does my PhilHealth cover for dependents?' by retrieving from uploaded policy and regulatory documents.

matches.rationale: The school is at a competitive disadvantage without knowing what peer institutions (corporate, international schools) offer. An AI research agent can autonomously gather and synthesize competitive benefits benchmarking data — scraping job postings, analyzing publicly available compensation surveys, and summarizing the gap. This gives leadership the evidence base needed to make the case for benefits investment.
matches.sketch: A competitive benefits benchmarking agent that continuously monitors job postings from rival schools and corporate employers, extracts stated benefits, and produces a quarterly gap report showing where the operator is losing the recruitment battle.

matches.rationale: The pain explicitly mentions retention challenges and school-hopping. With payroll, tenure, and exit interview data, a propensity model can identify which staff are at highest attrition risk due to benefits dissatisfaction — enabling targeted retention interventions before the resignation is submitted. This converts a passive benefits problem into a proactive retention tool.
matches.sketch: A staff attrition risk model that scores employees monthly on flight-risk probability, factoring in tenure, dependents, salary band, benefits uptake patterns, and peer benchmarks — surfacing at-risk staff to HR before they resign.

matches.rationale: Benefits opacity is an ongoing, multi-turn problem — staff need to ask follow-up questions, get personalized answers based on their employment status, and understand implications of different choices (e.g., HMO tier selection). A conversational agent can handle this complexity across multiple turns, maintaining context about the employee's situation, unlike static FAQs or one-shot RAG responses.
matches.sketch: An HR virtual assistant that guides staff through benefits enrollment, answers multi-turn eligibility questions, explains the gap versus corporate competitors, and flags when a staff member may be under-utilizing entitlements they already have.

painPoints.label: End-of-Service & Separation Settlements Disputes

matches.rationale: The root cause of disputes is the absence of clear, written policy on gratuity computation, unused leave payout, and separation tiers — an LLM can draft these policies from labor code inputs and operator-specific rules. For a multi-site education operator subject to DepEd and DOLE frameworks, generating legally-grounded separation policy templates, computation worksheets in prose, and individualized final settlement letters eliminates the ambiguity that triggers escalations.
matches.sketch: A policy-generation tool that takes labor law parameters (DOLE rules, DepEd portability rules, school employment tier) and outputs a complete End-of-Service Policy document and per-employee settlement computation narrative for HR approval and staff communication.

matches.rationale: HR officers at multi-site education operators must navigate overlapping sources — DOLE Labor Code, DepEd MOVs, individual school employment contracts, and internal policies — to answer 'what is this employee owed?' RAG grounds the answer in retrieved source documents rather than hallucinated figures, reducing computation errors and providing citable references that hold up if a case reaches DOLE. This directly addresses the compliance risk and delayed-final-payroll mechanisms.
matches.sketch: An HR knowledge assistant that lets staff query 'compute final pay for a Grade 6 teacher, 8 years tenure, voluntary resignation, with 14 unused leave days' and returns a step-by-step computation citing the exact policy clause and labor code article supporting each line item.

matches.rationale: Final settlement value is a continuous numeric quantity derived from tenure, salary, leave balance, employment classification, and applicable entitlement rules — a classic regression estimation problem. An AI-assisted computation engine removes human arithmetic error and standardizes how each input variable is weighted, directly addressing the disputed-computation mechanism and delayed final payroll caused by manual recalculation cycles.
matches.sketch: An automated settlement estimator that ingests HRIS fields (hire date, separation date, salary history, leave ledger, employment type) and outputs a line-item final pay computation with confidence bounds, replacing ad-hoc spreadsheets that vary by campus HR officer.

matches.rationale: The pain description explicitly mentions contingent liability recognized too late and cash flow shocks — leadership is blindsided by large settlements because no early-warning system exists. A risk model trained on employee tenure, employment type (end-of-contract vs permanent), accrued leave balance, and prior dispute history can score each departing employee's settlement as high/medium/low risk for DOLE escalation and flag cases for proactive resolution before they become formal complaints.
matches.sketch: A separation risk dashboard that scores every active notice-to-resign or contract-end event, surfaces the estimated liability range, and prompts HR to initiate pre-clearance conversations with high-risk cases — converting reactive firefighting into planned budget provisioning.

matches.rationale: The delayed final payroll problem is fundamentally a multi-step workflow failure: separation notice received → leave balance confirmed → salary arrears computed → gratuity calculated → approval obtained → payroll released. Each handoff introduces days of lag. A task automation agent can orchestrate this end-to-end — pulling data from HRIS, triggering computation, routing the approval chain, and issuing the final pay advice — within a defined SLA, eliminating the weeks-to-months delay that drives DOLE filings.
matches.sketch: An end-of-service workflow agent that activates on resignation or contract-end events, autonomously collects required data from connected HR and payroll systems, generates the computation document, routes it for dual approval, and releases payment instructions — with an audit trail admissible in DOLE proceedings.

painPoints.label: Blind Spot: No Attrition Root Cause Analysis

matches.rationale: Even when cohort data and exit patterns are available, leadership needs digestible narratives rather than raw tables. Summarization can condense a quarter's worth of attrition events — by campus, grade, or tenure band — into a concise board-ready briefing that highlights recurring themes and compares against prior periods. This addresses the specific pain that departures are treated as individual choices rather than institutional signals, by packaging the aggregate story in a format executives will actually read.
matches.sketch: A quarterly attrition briefing generator that ingests structured exit data and produces a 1-page narrative per campus — key drivers, trend vs. prior quarter, cohorts of concern — delivered automatically to principals and HR.

matches.rationale: Where exit interviews do exist (even as sparse email replies or manager notes), their free-text content contains the actual root causes — culture complaints, salary dissatisfaction, workload grievances, management conflicts. Text classification can automatically tag each exit record with one or more attrition driver categories, turning anecdotal text into structured signal. For an education operator with partial exit interview coverage, this converts previously lost qualitative data into a countable, trend-able dataset.
matches.sketch: An exit feedback classifier that reads any free-text departure note and assigns standardized driver tags (compensation, workload, management, career growth, relocation), building a structured attrition driver registry over time.

matches.rationale: Employee attrition likelihood is a canonical propensity modeling use case — the model ingests per-employee features (campus, tenure, role, performance, salary band, workload proxies) and outputs a churn probability score. For a multi-site education operator this directly addresses the inability to intervene before good staff leave, by surfacing at-risk individuals weeks or months before they exit. The data needed (HR records, payroll, scheduling) typically already exists in fragmented systems and can be unified without major new infrastructure.
matches.sketch: An attrition early-warning model that scores every employee monthly, flags high-risk staff to campus principals, and triggers a check-in workflow — replacing reactive rehiring with proactive retention outreach.

matches.rationale: Exit data today is anecdotal or stored in inconsistent formats — spreadsheets, email threads, paper forms, informal manager notes. Before any root-cause analysis can happen, this scattered semi-structured data must be normalized into a consistent schema (exit reason categories, campus, role, tenure bucket, date). AI-assisted ETL can map heterogeneous exit records to a unified schema without manual re-entry, making the historical signal recoverable. This is a prerequisite capability that unlocks cohort analysis.
matches.sketch: A one-time (and ongoing) ingestion pipeline that parses legacy exit records from varied formats into a standardized attrition database, enabling the first-ever cohort breakdowns by campus, discipline, and tenure.

matches.rationale: The current process breaks down because exit data collection is manual and inconsistently executed — when a staff member resigns, no one reliably triggers an exit interview, logs reasons, or updates the HR system. A task automation agent can orchestrate the full offboarding data-capture workflow: detecting a resignation event, sending a structured exit survey, chasing non-responses, parsing replies, and writing structured records to the HR system. This closes the data collection gap at the source rather than trying to recover lost signal after the fact.
matches.sketch: An automated offboarding workflow agent that fires on any resignation event, administers a short digital exit questionnaire, parses responses, and populates the attrition database — ensuring 100% exit data capture going forward without HR manual effort.

painPoints.label: Reactive Hiring vs. Proactive Workforce Planning

matches.rationale: Enrollment at each campus follows seasonal and multi-year trends that can be modeled from historical data. Forecasting enrollment trajectories per branch directly produces the staffing demand signal that is currently absent, enabling operators to see 6-18 months ahead rather than reacting to mid-year spikes. This is technically feasible with 3-5 years of historical enrollment records a 500-staff operator almost certainly holds.
matches.sketch: An enrollment-to-headcount forecasting module that ingests per-campus enrollment history and outputs monthly staffing demand projections by role category, flagging branches trending toward under- or over-staffing thresholds.

matches.rationale: A multi-year staffing roadmap is cited as absent — once quantitative forecasts exist, leadership still needs narrative plans, board briefings, and succession documentation that can be drafted rapidly from structured data. Text generation can convert headcount projections and gap analyses into readable roadmap documents, succession narratives, and role-profile briefs that currently do not exist.
matches.sketch: An automated workforce-planning narrative generator that converts tabular staffing gap data into board-ready multi-year roadmap documents and succession plan summaries per campus, reducing the hours required to produce planning artefacts from scratch.

matches.rationale: Unexpected departures are a root cause of reactive hiring; if the model can score individual staff members on attrition likelihood using features like tenure, role, campus performance, salary band, and absence patterns, operators gain the runway to develop successors or recruit before the seat goes empty. Education operators typically have HR data sufficient to train a basic attrition model.
matches.sketch: A staff attrition risk dashboard that flags employees with elevated departure probability each quarter, giving principals and HR 2-3 months of advance notice to initiate succession or back-fill pipelines.

matches.rationale: Once enrollment forecasts and attrition signals are available, the downstream problem is optimally assigning existing staff across campuses and planning new hires against budgeted headcount caps — a classic resource allocation problem. Multi-site operators face the added constraint of role-qualification requirements (e.g., STEM head credentials), making manual optimization error-prone and slow.
matches.sketch: A workforce allocation tool that takes enrollment forecasts and role-qualification constraints as inputs and outputs an optimal staff distribution plan across campuses, highlighting gaps that require external hiring versus internal transfers.

painPoints.label: Engagement Decline Undetected Until Exit

matches.rationale: Sentiment analysis and intent classification applied to open-text pulse survey responses, performance review comments, or anonymised internal communication can convert qualitative staff voice into structured engagement signals at scale. Multi-site education operators collect scattered text feedback (manager notes, grievance logs, appraisal comments) that currently goes unanalysed; classifying this text for sentiment, frustration themes (pay, workload, respect, autonomy), and urgency creates the early warning layer the org currently lacks.
matches.sketch: A lightweight pulse survey tool where open-text responses are automatically classified by sentiment and theme (workload, pay, management quality), aggregated by site and team, and surfaced as a weekly engagement heatmap for HR.

matches.rationale: Exit interview transcripts and long-form feedback contain rich signals about why staff leave, but HR teams at multi-site operators rarely have capacity to read and synthesise dozens of transcripts per quarter. Automated summarisation extracts recurring themes (e.g. 'workload inequity across sites', 'lack of promotion clarity') and produces site-level and role-level summaries that can inform targeted interventions — turning reactive post-departure data into forward-looking policy evidence.
matches.sketch: Automated exit interview synthesis that clusters departure reasons by site, role type, and tenure band, generating a quarterly retention briefing with specific, evidence-backed recommendations for leadership.

matches.rationale: Employee attrition prediction is a canonical propensity modeling task: given individual-level features (tenure, absenteeism, workload metrics, performance scores, pay band vs. market), the model outputs a churn probability per staff member. For a 500-staff multi-site operator, this converts the 'reactive HR scrambling' problem into a ranked watchlist that managers can act on before resignation letters arrive. The capability directly addresses the core pain mechanism — disengagement goes undetected because no signal is aggregated — by surfacing latent risk from data the org already holds.
matches.sketch: An attrition risk dashboard that scores every employee monthly using structured HR and operational data, flags high-risk individuals, and prompts a proactive retention conversation workflow for line managers.

matches.rationale: HR systems for a 500-staff operator hold structured signals of disengagement — increased sick days, declining utilisation, missed training completions, reduced extra-curricular participation — that can be combined into a multi-feature engagement risk classifier. This goes beyond propensity modeling (which outputs a probability) by categorising staff into actionable engagement segments (highly engaged, at risk, actively disengaged) that map directly to differentiated HR interventions.
matches.sketch: A segmentation model that classifies all staff into engagement tiers each month from structured HR data alone, enabling HR to prioritise outreach and tailor retention actions by cohort rather than treating all staff the same.

matches.rationale: A conversational pulse check agent replaces the anecdotal 'how's the team?' question with a structured, low-friction multi-turn dialogue that staff complete asynchronously via a messaging channel (Teams, WhatsApp, or internal portal). Multi-turn dialogue allows the agent to probe follow-up questions when a response signals concern — capturing richer engagement data than a static survey form while reducing the burden on managers who currently carry all informal sensing.
matches.sketch: A biweekly check-in bot sent to each staff member that asks 3-5 dynamic questions, adapts follow-ups based on responses, and feeds structured sentiment scores into a central HR dashboard without requiring manager mediation.

matches.rationale: The retention response workflow — detect a signal, escalate to the right manager, schedule a retention conversation, log the outcome, follow up — is a multi-step process that currently never starts because no signal exists. Once signals are generated (via propensity scoring or pulse surveys), an automation agent can orchestrate the end-to-end retention workflow: routing alerts to line managers, populating a conversation guide, logging outcomes in the HRIS, and scheduling follow-up reminders, closing the loop that currently breaks at detection.
matches.sketch: An automated retention workflow that triggers when an employee crosses an engagement-risk threshold: notifies the manager, generates a personalised conversation guide, and tracks whether a check-in occurred within 5 business days.

painPoints.label: Branch Principal Burnout & Leadership Vacuum

matches.rationale: A significant share of principal overload is administrative text work: staff communications, performance documentation, parent notices, HR correspondence, and compliance reports. AI text generation can draft these on behalf of the principal from structured inputs, directly reducing the workload contribution to burnout. This is technically trivial to deploy and requires no custom model — only prompt configuration aligned to school policy language.
matches.sketch: An AI writing assistant embedded in the school's admin portal that drafts routine HR letters, incident summaries, parent communications, and end-of-term reports from form inputs, freeing 3-5 hours per week per principal.

matches.rationale: Principals lacking dedicated HR support must navigate a fragmented landscape of policies, employment law obligations, incident procedures, and government funding requirements. RAG over the operator's policy corpus and HR documentation gives principals instant, cited answers to 'what do I do when…' questions, reducing the decision-paralysis and policy-search overhead that compounds workload stress. This is one of the highest-adoption AI patterns in education administration due to low perceived risk.
matches.sketch: A policy Q&A system where principals ask questions like 'What's our process for managing underperforming staff on a government contract?' and receive a grounded, cited response drawn from the operator's HR manual, employment agreements, and regulatory guidelines.

matches.rationale: Regional VPs stretched across 4-5 campuses cannot provide the day-to-day mentoring principals need. A conversational AI agent can serve as an always-available coaching and guidance resource for principals facing specific HR, staffing, or leadership challenges — surfacing policy guidance, decision frameworks, and escalation paths in natural dialogue. This directly addresses the 'cannot mentor principals' constraint without requiring VP bandwidth.
matches.sketch: A principal support chatbot trained on the operator's HR policies, leadership frameworks, and past incident resolutions that principals can consult 24/7 for HR decisions, conflict guidance, and performance conversation scripts — acting as a first-line mentor surrogate.

matches.rationale: The absence of a succession plan is partly a combinatorial problem: identifying which staff members satisfy the multi-dimensional constraints of a principal role (qualifications, seniority, performance history, willingness, availability) across multiple campuses simultaneously. Constraint satisfaction can map internal talent to succession slots, accounting for hard constraints (certifications, tenure) and soft constraints (cultural fit scores, cross-campus preferences). This is feasible with HR system data a 500+ staff operator already holds.
matches.sketch: A succession planning module that ingests staff profiles and principal role requirements, then generates a ranked shortlist of internal candidates per campus who satisfy succession constraints — giving Regional VPs a ready-made pipeline instead of reactive external recruitment.

matches.rationale: Principal burnout and impending departure follow observable behavioral patterns — reduced meeting attendance, declining performance review scores, increased leave requests, conflict escalations — that can be modeled at the individual level. A propensity model trained on historical principal and teacher attrition data can surface early-warning scores before a principal actively resigns. For a 500+ staff operator running 4-5 campuses per Regional VP, this directly addresses the 'leadership churn cascades' mechanism by enabling proactive intervention.
matches.sketch: A burnout/attrition propensity dashboard for Regional VPs that scores each principal weekly based on HR system signals (leave, incident reports, review cadence), flagging those above a risk threshold for targeted check-ins or workload relief.

matches.rationale: Principal overload is not just about drafting text — it involves multi-step administrative workflows: tracking staff leave requests, chasing compliance submissions, routing HR issues, and escalating incidents. A task automation agent can autonomously execute these workflows across the operator's systems (HRIS, LMS, email), reducing the cognitive overhead that accumulates into burnout. This directly targets the 'teaching + admin + HR' triple burden described in the pain point.
matches.sketch: An autonomous admin agent that monitors inbound HR requests, routes them to the correct process (leave approval, incident log, compliance checklist), sends follow-up reminders, and surfaces a daily briefing to the principal — cutting administrative context-switching by an estimated 40-60%.

painPoints.label: High Reliance on Contractors & Compliance Risk

matches.rationale: Each contractor engagement carries a distinct reclassification liability profile — tenure length, hours worked, functional overlap with permanent roles, and branch-level concentration all feed into DOLE audit exposure. AI risk scoring can quantify each contractor's reclassification probability and aggregate branch-level liability, giving legal and HR leadership a ranked list of the highest-exposure relationships. For a 500-staff multi-site operator with 40-50% contractor payroll, this directly addresses the compliance risk that is otherwise invisible until an audit.
matches.sketch: A contractor compliance risk scoring system that ingests payroll, attendance, and role data per contractor, outputs a reclassification likelihood score and severity estimate, and surfaces the top-risk engagements across all branches in a weekly dashboard for the HR/legal team.

matches.rationale: When a DOLE audit or internal review is triggered, generating accurate, consistent documentation — contractor status justifications, engagement summaries, and compliance narratives — is time-consuming and error-prone when done manually across dozens of branches. LLM-based generation can produce audit-ready contractor classification rationales and HR policy communications at scale, reducing the legal exposure from poorly worded or inconsistent records. This also supports staff communication: generating clear, empathetic messaging about contractor vs. permanent distinctions helps address morale issues.
matches.sketch: An AI documentation tool that takes structured contractor record data and generates audit-ready classification justification letters, branch compliance summaries, and internal HR policy communications tailored to each contractor's situation.

matches.rationale: Compliance auditing across 500+ staff and multiple contractor categories is a multi-step workflow — pulling payroll records, cross-referencing hours and role descriptions against DOLE thresholds, flagging anomalies, escalating to branch managers, and logging remediation actions. An AI task automation agent can orchestrate this entire pipeline across all branches on a recurring schedule, replacing what is currently either manual spot-checking or not done at all. The 60%+ annual contractor turnover means this workflow must run continuously, not just at audit time.
matches.sketch: A recurring compliance automation agent that ingests payroll and HR system data monthly, applies DOLE classification rules, generates per-branch risk flags and remediation tasks, and routes action items to branch managers with deadline tracking.

matches.rationale: With 60%+ annual turnover among contractors, predicting which individuals are likely to leave in the next 30-60 days allows branch managers to proactively extend offers, adjust workloads, or pre-recruit replacements — reducing the institutional knowledge loss and student continuity disruptions the pain point describes. Features like tenure, engagement frequency, hours worked, and cross-site assignment patterns can predict departure propensity at the individual contractor level. This is technically feasible with the HR/payroll data a 500-staff operator would already have.
matches.sketch: A contractor churn prediction model that scores each active contractor on attrition likelihood monthly, surfacing high-risk departures to branch HR coordinators with a recommended retention or succession action.

matches.rationale: Contractors who are behaviorally indistinguishable from permanent employees — same hours, same supervisory relationships, same long tenure — represent the reclassification risk that DOLE audits target. Anomaly detection on structured contractor engagement data can flag engagements that deviate from typical contractor patterns (short-term, project-scoped, irregular hours) and look instead like disguised employment. This surfaces misclassification risk that rule-based systems miss because the thresholds are fuzzy and branch-specific.
matches.sketch: An anomaly detection layer on contractor payroll and attendance data that continuously flags engagements exhibiting permanent-employee behavioral signatures, triggering an HR review workflow before they accumulate DOLE audit exposure.

matches.rationale: The root cause of over-reliance on contractors is an under-optimized staffing mix — branches hire contractors to fill permanent headcount gaps because the cost model appears favorable short-term, ignoring the compliance, quality, and continuity costs. AI-driven workforce optimization can model the true cost (including turnover, training loss, and reclassification liability) of different contractor-to-permanent ratios across branches and recommend a staffing allocation that reduces compliance exposure while staying within budget. This shifts the decision from reactive to strategic.
matches.sketch: A workforce composition optimizer that models total cost of ownership for contractor vs. permanent staff at each branch (including DOLE risk, turnover cost, and quality impact), and recommends a target headcount mix with a transition plan for each site.

painPoints.label: Fragmented Onboarding Across Campuses

matches.rationale: The core absence driving this pain is a standardized onboarding playbook — contracts, policy documents, role expectation briefs, IT setup checklists, and campus-specific welcome guides all need to exist but currently don't. Text generation can produce first-draft versions of every document type from structured templates, with campus-specific variables filled in. This directly attacks the 'each campus improvises' root cause.
matches.sketch: A playbook generator that takes campus name, role type, and start date as inputs and produces a complete, consistent onboarding packet — welcome letter, role brief, checklist, policy summary — ready for HR review and distribution.

matches.rationale: Even when onboarding documents exist in scattered forms across campuses, staff cannot find the right version for their situation. A RAG system over the existing policy corpus — contracts, IT guides, payroll forms, campus handbooks — lets HR staff and new hires ask natural-language questions and get grounded, citation-backed answers. This is a realistic first step for an operator that has documents but no centralized access to them.
matches.sketch: An internal knowledge base chatbot that indexes all onboarding materials from every campus and answers questions like 'What IT access does a part-time instructor need at the Westside campus?' with direct document citations.

matches.rationale: The onboarding failure is a multi-step workflow failure: system access provisioning, payroll registration, campus assignment, role briefing, and document delivery all need to happen in sequence across multiple systems. A task automation agent can orchestrate these steps — triggering IT provisioning, sending policy documents, confirming payroll registration, and flagging blockers — without manual coordination from HR at each campus. The 30% early attrition figure signals the cost of these gaps is measurable and acute.
matches.sketch: An onboarding orchestration agent that triggers on a new hire record, dispatches system access requests, sends the correct document set per campus and role type, tracks completion of each step, and escalates uncompleted items before day one.

matches.rationale: New hires — especially temp and contract staff — face first-week confusion because there is no single place to ask questions about their role, campus, systems, or policies. A conversational agent can serve as an always-available onboarding guide that answers FAQs, surfaces the right documents, and routes escalations to the correct HR contact per campus. This directly addresses the 'unclear role expectations' pain mechanism without requiring HR bandwidth at each site.
matches.sketch: A new-hire chatbot deployed via Slack or a web portal, pre-loaded with campus-specific policies, role briefs, and IT setup guides, that walks each hire through their first week and hands off unresolved issues to the right person.

matches.rationale: Faculty hired for HQ without a clear campus assignment at start represents a matching/assignment problem: each hire has a role, qualifications, and preferences; each campus has headcount needs, schedule constraints, and role requirements. A constraint satisfaction system can produce valid assignments that satisfy both sides, replacing the ad-hoc 'figure it out after they arrive' process that creates first-week ambiguity.
matches.sketch: A campus assignment tool that takes a new hire's profile and available openings across campuses and produces an optimal, constraint-satisfying assignment before the start date — eliminating the 'unclear campus assignment' gap entirely.

matches.rationale: The 30% early attrition rate within 90 days for temp/contract staff is a measurable outcome that a propensity model can predict at the individual level. Features like campus assignment clarity, document completion rate, system access timing, and role type can be used to flag hires at elevated flight risk before they disengage. This turns a lagging indicator (attrition) into an actionable early warning.
matches.sketch: A 90-day attrition risk model that scores each new hire during their first two weeks based on onboarding completion signals, and triggers proactive check-ins from managers for high-risk individuals.

painPoints.label: Weak Local Talent Pipeline & Referrals

matches.rationale: The organization spends heavily on job boards and agencies partly because they lack compelling, tailored outreach materials and partnership proposals. AI text generation can produce role-specific job postings, referral program communications, and partnership pitch letters to teacher training colleges — content that currently requires manual effort or is simply absent. For niche roles like STEM and special education, well-crafted targeted outreach reduces time-to-fill without agency dependency.
matches.sketch: An AI-assisted content toolkit that auto-generates job postings, referral program invite emails, and partnership proposals to local teacher colleges, parameterized by role type and campus location.

matches.rationale: The organization has no structured way to surface past applicants, alumni, or internal candidates who could fill current openings — meaning every vacancy triggers a fresh expensive search. Semantic search over a candidate database (even a modest ATS export) allows recruiters to match job descriptions against prior applicants by skills and experience rather than exact keyword overlap, directly reducing agency dependency for repeat or similar roles.
matches.sketch: A semantic candidate matching layer on top of the existing ATS or spreadsheet-based applicant archive, letting HR enter a role description and retrieve ranked prior applicants with relevant profiles.

matches.rationale: The organization lacks partnerships with nearby teacher training colleges and has no intelligence on local talent supply. A research agent can autonomously map teacher training programs in each campus's service area, identify relevant department contacts, compile program graduation timelines, and surface partnership precedents — giving HR a ready-made outreach list that currently doesn't exist. This directly attacks the 'limited partnerships with teacher training colleges' root cause.
matches.sketch: A research agent that, given a campus location, produces a structured report of nearby teacher-training institutions, their specializations (STEM, special ed), graduate output volumes, and suggested partnership entry points.

matches.rationale: High recruitment costs are partly driven by reactive hiring — roles are posted only when they become vacant. Predicting which staff are at elevated attrition risk allows HR to pre-activate the local pipeline and referral programs before a role opens, reducing time-to-fill and cutting agency dependency for niche roles where reactive sourcing is most expensive.
matches.sketch: An employee attrition propensity model trained on tenure, role type, campus, and engagement signals that flags at-risk staff 60-90 days before likely departure, triggering early pipeline activation.

matches.rationale: Building a local talent pipeline requires matching candidate profiles (availability, qualifications, location, specialization) against open roles across multiple campuses while satisfying certification and regulatory constraints. This is fundamentally a matching/assignment problem — the kind where constraint satisfaction excels — and it applies directly to the graduate trainee and alumni coaching programs the organization lacks.
matches.sketch: A trainee placement engine that matches graduating teacher-college candidates or internal staff seeking upskilling to open roles and development slots across campuses, respecting credential requirements and location constraints.

painPoints.label: Inter-Campus Staff Allocation & Mobility

matches.rationale: This is the most direct match: the pain is exactly about assigning limited staff (who, what role) to campuses (where) given capacity constraints. An AI optimizer can ingest headcount data across 10-15 campuses, identify surplus/deficit by role, and recommend redeployment or cross-campus assignments — replacing the ad-hoc principal-to-principal phone calls. For a 500+ staff operator, the constraint space (role types, qualifications, contract terms, travel distance) is tractable for standard optimization models.
matches.sketch: A centralized staffing optimization tool that ingests campus-level roster and enrollment data, then surfaces weekly redeployment recommendations (e.g., 'Campus B has 2 surplus admin; Campus F needs 1 math teacher — Teacher X is qualified and within 10km'). Principals review and approve; the system logs decisions and tracks mobility over time.

matches.rationale: A secondary but real use case: once a redeployment recommendation is generated, someone must communicate it persuasively to the receiving principal and the staff member. AI text generation can draft the inter-campus transfer proposal, the staff communication explaining the opportunity, and the central HR summary report — reducing the administrative writing burden that currently falls on overloaded principals or HR staff.
matches.sketch: Automated drafting of transfer communications and staffing summary reports: given structured data (staff profile, campus need, proposed dates), the system generates a ready-to-send email to the staff member and a one-page summary for the receiving principal. Saves 30-60 minutes per redeployment event.

matches.rationale: The approval delay pain is partly a workflow problem: requests go principal-to-principal informally, then escalate to central HR manually. A task automation agent can own the inter-campus request workflow end-to-end — receiving a shortage notification, checking the centralized staffing view, drafting a redeployment proposal, routing it for approval, and updating rosters across systems once approved. This removes the manual coordination bottleneck without requiring principals to change how they flag needs.
matches.sketch: An automated staffing request pipeline: principal submits a shortage flag via a simple form, the agent checks available staff across all campuses, generates a proposed redeployment with justification, routes to HR and the relevant principal for approval, then updates HRIS records on confirmation. Target: reduce time-to-resolution from weeks to 48 hours.

matches.rationale: The chronic understaffing at branches is partly a mobility problem — staff reluctance to transfer. A propensity model can predict which staff members are most likely to accept a mobility offer based on features like tenure, commute patterns, past transfer history, role type, and engagement indicators. This lets HR target redeployment conversations at staff who are realistically movable, rather than blanket requests that generate resistance and delay.
matches.sketch: An employee mobility propensity score surfaced inside the HR dashboard: for any open inter-campus need, the system ranks eligible staff by likelihood to accept a transfer and flags the top candidates for a proactive conversation. Reduces the approval-delay frustration by front-loading feasibility assessment.

matches.rationale: Inter-campus allocation is fundamentally a matching/assignment problem with multiple simultaneous hard constraints: staff qualifications, contract type, union/employment rules, commute radius, minimum staffing ratios per campus, and soft constraints like staff preferences. Constraint satisfaction models handle this class of problem directly, especially when simple priority rules fail because constraints conflict (e.g., the only surplus qualified teacher is on a part-time contract that doesn't allow inter-campus work).
matches.sketch: A constraint-satisfaction engine that takes the full set of staffing rules, staff profiles, and campus needs as inputs and outputs a valid (or near-valid) allocation configuration — flagging constraint violations rather than silently producing infeasible plans. Could run nightly or on-demand when a campus raises a shortage flag.

painPoints.label: Timetable Conflicts & Subject Specialization Mismatches

matches.rationale: Before scheduling, each proposed teacher-subject assignment can be classified as qualified, underqualified, or misaligned based on structured teacher profile data (certifications, specializations, experience). This pre-validation step directly addresses the pain of teachers being forced into misaligned loads by flagging mismatches in the draft schedule before it is released.
matches.sketch: An assignment qualification checker that scores every teacher-subject pairing in a draft schedule as compliant, borderline, or non-compliant, with reasons — integrated into the schedule build workflow so conflicts surface before publication rather than after staff receive rosters.

matches.rationale: Timetabling is a canonical constraint satisfaction problem: teacher qualifications, campus availability, subject requirements, load limits, and student cohort needs must all be simultaneously satisfied. This directly addresses the pain mechanism — schedules built without cross-campus visibility produce constraint violations that take 15-20 days to remediate manually. A constraint solver can validate feasibility before schedule release, eliminating reactive churn.
matches.sketch: A timetable validation and generation tool that ingests teacher specialty profiles, campus availability, and subject requirements across all sites, then produces or validates schedules that satisfy all hard constraints (qualifications) and optimizes soft constraints (load balance, travel). Flags conflicts before schedule release.

matches.rationale: The core pain is that specialist teachers (STEM, Special Ed) are not optimally assigned across campuses — a direct resource allocation problem. AI scheduling can model specialist availability as a constrained resource pool and assign them across sites to meet coverage requirements, replacing the manual campus-by-campus approach that creates misalignment and rushed hires.
matches.sketch: A cross-campus resource allocation engine that treats specialist teachers as shared resources, automatically distributing them across sites based on subject demand, qualification match, and contracted hours — surfacing gaps weeks in advance so hiring can be planned rather than reactive.

matches.rationale: Employee attrition propensity modeling can identify which teachers are most likely to disengage or leave when assigned outside their specialty — quantifying the morale risk of specific scheduling decisions. This gives leadership an early signal on which misalignment scenarios are highest risk before finalizing the schedule.
matches.sketch: A morale-risk scoring model that predicts which teachers are at elevated attrition risk when assigned outside specialty, integrated into the scheduling review so planners can prioritize resolving high-risk mismatches first.

matches.rationale: The 15-20 day post-release iteration cycle involves repetitive multi-step workflows: identifying conflicts, notifying affected teachers, sourcing alternatives, updating rosters across systems, and communicating changes to parents. An automation agent can handle the orchestration of these steps — pulling conflict data, drafting notifications, and updating scheduling systems — compressing the remediation cycle significantly.
matches.sketch: A schedule-remediation automation agent that, when a conflict is flagged, autonomously identifies available qualified alternatives, drafts staff and parent communications, and queues system updates for human approval — reducing the 15-20 day iteration to a same-day triage workflow.

painPoints.label: Payroll Admin Overhead & Multi-Campus Compliance

matches.rationale: Many campuses still use paper timesheets or scanned spreadsheet printouts. OCR and form-field extraction can convert these into structured digital records, eliminating the manual re-keying step that drives the 2-3 day reconciliation cycle. For a multi-site operator with fragmented legacy systems, this is often the unavoidable first step before any downstream automation is possible.
matches.sketch: A document ingestion pipeline that scans uploaded timesheet images or PDFs, extracts employee ID, hours, dates, and shift codes, and outputs a normalized CSV or JSON payload ready for payroll processing — removing manual data entry across all campuses.

matches.rationale: DepEd compliance reporting requires narrative summaries of workforce data each period. Generating these structured compliance reports and internal HR communications (e.g., leave dispute resolution letters, pay discrepancy notifications) from structured payroll data is a well-scoped text generation task that reduces manual drafting burden on HR staff.
matches.sketch: Automated generation of DepEd-format compliance reports and campus-facing HR communications from finalized payroll data, parameterized per campus and pay period — saving HR staff hours of templated writing each cycle.

matches.rationale: The core technical problem is that campuses have inconsistent schema definitions — 'full day', late allowance thresholds, and pay period calendars differ per site. AI-assisted data transformation can map each campus's local timesheet format and shift taxonomy to a canonical master schema, resolving definitional mismatches before payroll runs. This directly addresses the normalization overhead that causes 2-3 day reconciliation cycles.
matches.sketch: A schema normalization layer that ingests raw time and attendance exports from each campus (regardless of format or field naming), applies campus-specific mapping rules, and outputs a unified payroll-ready dataset — reducing reconciliation from days to minutes.

matches.rationale: Recurring leave accrual disputes and compliance discrepancies are often triggered by edge-case entries — unusual overtime patterns, duplicate clock-ins, or leave codes applied inconsistently across campuses. Anomaly detection on the normalized time-and-attendance data can flag these outliers before payroll is finalized, shifting from reactive dispute resolution to proactive exception handling.
matches.sketch: An anomaly scoring module that runs over each pay cycle's time records and surfaces flagged rows (e.g., duplicate entries, out-of-range hours, leave codes inconsistent with campus policy) for HR review before payroll is submitted — reducing dispute volume and DepEd compliance risk.

matches.rationale: The full payroll reconciliation workflow spans multiple steps: ingesting data from each campus, normalizing formats, applying policy rules (shift definitions, leave accruals), flagging exceptions, and generating DepEd-compliant reports. A task automation agent can orchestrate this multi-step pipeline end-to-end, including routing exceptions to the right approver and triggering downstream actions — replacing the current manual 2-3 day process.
matches.sketch: An end-to-end payroll reconciliation agent that ingests campus timesheet exports, applies normalization and policy rules, runs anomaly detection, generates exception reports for HR sign-off, and produces DepEd compliance summaries — compressing the pay cycle close from days to hours.

painPoints.label: Substitute & Absence Coverage Friction

matches.rationale: The core pain is the absence of a dynamic substitute pool with real-time availability visibility and automated assignment. Resource allocation and scheduling directly addresses this by optimally matching available substitute staff to open class slots given constraints like qualifications, subject area, campus proximity, and prior engagement history. For a 500+ staff multi-site operator, this replaces ad-hoc phone-tree scrambling with an automated coverage engine.
matches.sketch: A substitute coverage platform that ingests real-time absence notifications, queries a pool of pre-vetted substitutes by availability and fit, and proposes ranked assignments to principals — reducing response time from hours to minutes.

matches.rationale: The current process is a multi-step manual workflow: detect absence → identify available subs → contact them → confirm → notify affected staff. A task automation agent can execute this entire chain autonomously — receiving absence triggers, querying substitute availability, sending outreach, logging confirmations, and updating timetable systems — without principal intervention. This directly eliminates the ad-hoc scrambling described in the pain point.
matches.sketch: An autonomous coverage agent that fires on absence submission, identifies and contacts the best-fit available substitute via SMS/email, confirms acceptance, and updates the school's scheduling system — all before the principal needs to act.

matches.rationale: One identified sub-problem is that online or external substitutes have no curriculum continuity context. Text generation can auto-produce substitute briefing notes from existing lesson plans, learning management system records, and class notes — giving any sub immediate, actionable context without teacher preparation time.
matches.sketch: An auto-briefing tool that generates a one-page substitute handover note (current unit, student needs flags, lesson plan summary, classroom norms) pulled from the LMS each time a coverage gap is confirmed.

matches.rationale: A meaningful portion of the scrambling cost comes from last-minute absences that were not anticipated. Propensity modeling can score individual teachers' likelihood of calling in absent on a given day using historical absence patterns, seasonal factors, and workload signals — giving operations teams advance warning to pre-activate substitute outreach before a gap materializes.
matches.sketch: An absence-risk scoring model that flags teachers with elevated absence probability 24-48 hours ahead, prompting proactive substitute pre-booking and reducing the proportion of truly last-minute coverage crises.

painPoints.label: Ambiguous Contract Types & Deployment Rights

matches.rationale: HR staff and principals constantly need to know whether a specific contract type permits cross-campus redeployment, what constitutes an involuntary transfer under a given contract, and what the applicable policy is. RAG over the operator's HR policy corpus, employment contracts, and labor regulations can surface grounded, citation-backed answers instantly rather than escalating to HR or legal. This directly addresses the 'no transparent policy codified in system' pain and the principal uncertainty on deployment rights.
matches.sketch: An internal HR policy Q&A tool where principals type natural-language questions ('Can I redeploy a probationary teacher from Campus A to Campus B?') and receive answers grounded in the actual policy documents, contracts, and labor code provisions, with citations.

matches.rationale: The absence of a codified, human-readable policy is itself a root cause of disputes. AI can draft clear, plain-language policy documents that define deployment rights by contract type, define 'involuntary transfer,' and map out grievance procedures — work that HR teams perpetually defer. For a 500+ staff operator with mixed contract types, getting this policy authored and maintained is a direct pain reducer.
matches.sketch: An AI-assisted policy drafting workflow where HR inputs contract type parameters and labor code references, and the system generates a structured Deployment Rights Policy document ready for legal review and codification into the HR system.

matches.rationale: Each employee record has a contract type, term dates, campus assignment, and deployment clauses buried in unstructured contract documents. Extracting these as structured key-value pairs (contract_type, deployment_scope, redeployment_consent_clause, term_end_date) from the raw documents creates the data foundation needed to surface deployment rights at scale and prevent disputes. Without this structured extraction, no downstream policy-enforcement or search solution can function.
matches.sketch: A pipeline that ingests all employee contract PDFs, extracts structured contract attributes, and populates a master HR database with deployment-rights fields that principals and HR can query before making assignment decisions.

matches.rationale: When principals need to fill a vacancy or reorganize staffing across campuses, the valid assignment options are constrained by contract type, deployment scope, consent requirements, and labor rules simultaneously. A constraint-satisfaction layer can enumerate which staff are legally deployable to which campuses under which conditions, reducing principal guesswork and pre-empting grievances before a transfer is initiated.
matches.sketch: A staffing assignment tool where a principal inputs a role vacancy at Campus B, and the system returns a list of eligible staff from across the network with each person's deployment eligibility status and any required steps (consent form, HR approval), derived from contract constraint rules.
